findings.location: Execution Model section: BYOK-local bullet (lines 68-73) and the mode-switch sentence (lines 89-91). One file, one section, two adjacent claims about how/where execution happens.
findings.issue: The flagship vision doc describes a simpler and partly-incorrect execution model than the canonical one. (a) Lines 68-73 say BYOK-local means "LLM API calls go directly from the user's machine to the providers" with no qualification — true at the machine boundary, but it erases the decided desktop design where the authenticated HTTPS egress is DELEGATED to the Rust `llm_stream` command so the raw key never enters the WebView (desktop-architecture.md lines 78, 99-118; local-first-and-security.md lines 41-53, 94-104). (b) Lines 89-91 attribute ALL THREE modes (local, managed, cloud) to a switch "behind the `LLMProvider` seam." That is right for local and managed (managed = a new `ManagedGatewayProvider` behind LLMProvider per ADR-0012 and roadmap/README.md line 73), but WRONG for cloud: cloud execution is selected at the `ExecutionHost`/`CloudExecutionHost` seam and moves the whole engine server-side (roadmap/README.md lines 71-74; execution-model.md lines 148-154). Cloud changes WHERE the engine runs, not which LLMProvider is injected.
findings.fix: (a) Keep the user-facing point (calls leave the user's own machine straight to the provider; nothing transits a Relavium server) but add a half-sentence/footnote: "on the desktop the authenticated HTTPS call is performed by the Tauri Rust core, so the raw key never enters the WebView; on CLI/VS Code it is a direct in-process fetch — either way no traffic transits a Relavium server." Cross-link architecture/desktop-architecture.md#llm-egress-is-rust-delegated-on-the-desktop. (b) Split the seam claim: local + managed switch behind the `LLMProvider` seam; cloud execution is the separate `ExecutionHost`/`CloudExecutionHost` seam that relocates the engine. Match roadmap/README.md lines 71-74 and execution-model.md.

findings.location: Notes on the Choices, "Keys never touch the frontend" bullet, lines 53-55.
findings.issue: "API keys live in the OS keychain and are resolved by the backend at call time" is presented as a universal stack property, but the frontend/backend split only exists on the desktop surface (the privileged Rust `llm_stream` command). On the CLI, the VS Code extension host, and the Phase-2 Bun API there is NO separate backend — those surfaces resolve the key and make the call inside the one trusted JS process that also runs the engine (local-first-and-security.md lines 51-53; desktop-architecture.md lines 102-108). The phrase imports the desktop WebView/Rust split as a stack-wide invariant; the genuine cross-surface invariant is narrower.
findings.fix: Reword to the actual invariant: "Keys live only in the OS keychain, are resolved at call time, and never enter the WebView renderer or any log/checkpoint. On the desktop the keychain read + HTTPS egress are delegated to the Rust core; on CLI/VS Code/Bun the key is resolved inside the single trusted process." Link keychain-and-secrets.md and desktop-architecture.md.

findings.location: "Cloud execution is Phase 2" section, lines 60-63: "...must support local, cloud, and (Phase 2) managed modes via a clean interface switch".
findings.issue: The parenthetical marks ONLY `managed` as Phase 2, which reads as though `cloud` is Phase 1 — directly contradicting this same file's section heading "Cloud execution is Phase 2" two lines above, the canonical mode table in ADR-0012 (cloud = Phase 2, BYOK-cloud), the glossary lines 163-166 ("cloud ... Phase 2"), and deployment-models.md lines 33, 55 (BYOK-central `cloud` is Phase 2). Both `cloud` and `managed` are Phase 2; only `local` is Phase 1. The constraint mis-scopes the phasing of the very modes it enumerates.
findings.fix: Change to: "...must support `local` (Phase 1) plus `cloud` and `managed` (both Phase 2) via a clean interface switch..." so the phase marking matches ADR-0012, the glossary, and deployment-models.md.

findings.location: "Channel / IPC stream" entry, lines 105-107: "...used to stream run events from the backend to the canvas. Carries the same events that SSE carries in Phase 2."
findings.issue: The glossary defines the desktop Channel as streaming run events "from the backend to the canvas," implying the engine runs in the Rust backend and pushes RunEvents over IPC. The canonical model is the opposite: the engine + RunEventBus run WebView-side, so most run events never cross IPC at all (desktop-architecture.md lines 142-147: "many run events do not need to cross the IPC boundary ... the engine's RunEventBus is in the same JS runtime as the stores"). On the desktop the only thing crossing Rust→WebView on the LLM hot path is a Channel<StreamChunk> for the delegated egress (desktop-architecture.md lines 36-44, 78, 99-118). The definition reverses where execution lives and overstates what the IPC Channel carries.
findings.fix: Redefine: "the typed, backpressure-aware Tauri v2 channel. On the desktop it carries the StreamChunk frames of the Rust-delegated LLM egress back to the WebView adapter; the engine's RunEventBus runs WebView-side, so most run events never cross IPC. The cross-surface event union is the same one SSE carries in Phase 2." Cite desktop-architecture.md and execution-model.md. (Note: sse-event-schema.md line 5 and execution-model.md line 86 do show a Tauri Channel<RunEvent> for the high-throughput token stream, so the channel does carry run-event payloads on the desktop — keep that, but stop implying the engine/backend is the source of those events.)

findings.location: Small-team row (line 54) + Team-tier prose (lines 130-143), vs reference/portal/api-reference.md Team tier (line 146).
findings.issue: The two canonical homes disagree on the Team tier's DEFAULT key model. deployment-models.md sells Team as "BYOK-local per seat (default)" and lists the central key vault as a Phase-2 add-on (line 54: "Team tier (central vault, RBAC, budgets): Phase 2"). But api-reference.md line 146 frames the Team tier's BYOK column as "the team pays its own providers via a central BYOK key vault (org key vault)" — i.e. BYOK-central / `cloud`-mode (server-side vault). Since the central vault is BYOK-central = `cloud` = Phase 2 (ADR-0012; glossary lines 147-151), api-reference.md effectively makes the Team tier depend on a Phase-2 cloud-mode capability, while deployment-models.md ships Team as fundamentally BYOK-local-per-seat. A reader picking a tier cannot tell which key model Team actually ships with.
findings.fix: Align the two homes (one-canonical-home rule): in api-reference.md's Team row, make the central org key vault an OPTIONAL Phase-2 capability with BYOK-local-per-seat + git-shared YAML as the default (matching deployment-models.md); or, if Team genuinely centers on a central vault, update deployment-models.md to say so. Pick one and have the other cite it.

findings.location: Proof Point 1 (lines 15-16) and Statement (lines 8-11); mirrored in vision.md "Why It Wins" (lines 95-97) vs the Four Surfaces table (vision.md line 61) and project-structure.md line 53.
findings.issue: The positioning slides between "owns all four surfaces" and "the identical engine runs on all four." uvp.md Proof Point 1 says "owns all four surfaces (desktop, VS Code, CLI, web) with a single workflow runtime" and vision.md lines 95-97 says "runs the identical engine across desktop, VS Code, CLI, and (Phase 2) the cloud." But the fourth surface is the web portal, which vision.md line 61 and project-structure.md line 53 both define as a CONTROL PLANE that explicitly does NOT execute workflows ("NOT where workflows execute — it is not an execution plane"). The "identical engine runs on all four" claim is not true of the portal; the Phase-2 engine-on-cloud story is the cloud-EXECUTION worker plane (apps/api workers), a different thing than the portal counted in the four surfaces.
findings.fix: Separate the two claims consistently: (1) the engine runs identically on three Phase-1 execution surfaces (desktop, VS Code, CLI) and, in Phase 2, on cloud-execution workers — that is the "one runtime" story; (2) the web portal is a fourth SURFACE but a control plane, not an execution surface. Reword vision.md "Why It Wins" and uvp.md Proof Point 1 so the runtime claim attaches to the executing surfaces + cloud workers, while the portal is counted as a surface without implying it runs the engine.

findings.location: Missing entry in the "Execution & Runtime" or "Data, Files & Storage" sections (the cost-unit term that the SSE/cost entries at lines 108-114 implicitly rely on). Canonical sources: reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md line 86 and reference/desktop/database-schema.md line 50.
findings.issue: The canonical cost unit (integer micro-cents, used in `costMicrocents`/`cumulativeCostMicrocents` and the per-node cost waterfall that vision.md and uvp.md market) has NO glossary entry, despite the glossary's stated job of "one canonical definition each" and cost attribution being a marketed killer feature. Worse, the unit's value is defined inconsistently across the two reference homes — a confirmed 100x discrepancy: sse-event-schema.md line 86 defines `costMicrocents` as "integer μ¢ (1e-8 USD)" while database-schema.md line 50 defines the money type as "INTEGER micro-cents (USD x 1,000,000)" = 1e-6 USD. Same term, two different magnitudes, no canonical home to anchor either.
findings.fix: Add a glossary entry (Execution/Runtime or Data): "Cost (micro-cent) — costs are tracked as integer micro-cents to avoid float rounding; see sse-event-schema.md and database-schema.md." Separately flag for the reference owners to reconcile the 1e-8-USD (sse-event-schema.md line 86) vs 1e-6-USD / USDx1,000,000 (database-schema.md line 50) definitions to a single canonical magnitude before either is treated as stable.

report.overallVerdict: Consolidated 7 deduped findings (from the verified product-layer set you transmitted). NOTE ON COMPLETENESS: the CONFIRMED input array reaching me was truncated mid-finding (it cut off inside the vision.md LLMProvider-vs-ExecutionHost item). Only the 9 product-domain findings arrived; the architecture- and standards-domain findings needed to total the stated 21 confirmed were NOT in my input, so they are not represented here — I did not fabricate them. The two vision.md execution-model items (\"calls go directly to providers\" + \"LLMProvider seam covers all three modes\") were merged into one finding since they are the same file/section. All 7 are grounded against the canonical sources I read: desktop-architecture.md, local-first-and-security.md, execution-model.md, roadmap/README.md, deployment-models.md, api-reference.md, sse-event-schema.md, database-schema.md, glossary.md. Net theme: the product/positioning docs (vision, uvp, tech-stack, product-constraints, glossary) repeatedly describe the LLM-egress/execution model more simply or differently than the decided canonical model — Rust-delegated egress on desktop, distinct LLMProvider vs ExecutionHost seams, and cloud+managed both being Phase 2 — plus two genuine cross-home contradictions (Team-tier key model; the micro-cent magnitude 1e-8 vs 1e-6). Highest-leverage fixes: product-constraints.md phasing parenthetical (medium), the deployment-models vs api-reference Team-tier key-model conflict (medium), and the vision.md execution-model/seam accuracy (medium). To produce the full 21-finding consolidation, re-send the complete CONFIRMED array including the architecture and standards entries.
